prompt:
Read the following reasoning trace carefully. Your task is to analyze whether
it contains any cognitive biases.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"has_bias": true/false, "bias_name": "name of bias or none", "explanation": "how the bias manifests in this reasoning"}

--- Reasoning Trace ---
We're evaluating the new drug screening protocol. The trial data shows a 95%
accuracy rate for detecting true positives, which is impressive. With that
level of accuracy, I think we should implement it as a standard screening
tool for all new admissions. The cost per test is reasonable at $45, and
catching 95 out of 100 cases early would save significant downstream
treatment costs. I'm writing up the recommendation to the medical director
with a strong endorsement. A 95% detection rate puts this among the best
screening tools available for this condition.
--- End Trace ---
response: {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"duration_ms":5860,"duration_api_ms":5686,"num_turns":1,"result":"```json\n{\"has_bias\": true, \"bias_name\": \"Base Rate Neglect (also: neglect of prior probability / false positive fallacy)\", \"explanation\": \"The reasoning focuses entirely on the 95% sensitivity (true positive rate) while ignoring the base rate of the condition in the screened population. In any low-prevalence condition, even a 95% sensitivity combined with an unspecified specificity can produce a majority of false positives. For example, if prevalence is 1% and specificity is 95%, roughly half of all positive tests would be false positives. Without knowing the base rate and specificity, the 95% figure is insufficient to justify a blanket 'strong endorsement' — yet the reasoning treats it as decisive evidence of value.\"}\n```","stop_reason":"end_turn","session_id":"b7e5978b-4d6b-4d67-953e-f98c406e5a78","total_cost_usd":0.042094349999999996,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":9633,"cache_read_input_tokens":11182,"output_tokens":174,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":9633},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"},"modelUsage":{"claude-sonnet-4-6":{"inputTokens":2,"outputTokens":174,"cacheReadInputTokens":11182,"cacheCreationInputTokens":9633,"webSearchRequests":0,"costUSD":0.042094349999999996,"contextWindow":200000,"maxOutputTokens":32000}},"permission_denials":[],"terminal_reason":"completed","fast_mode_state":"off","uuid":"18948504-8baa-468c-afb4-892708e9e673"}

parsed.bias_name: Base Rate Neglect (also: neglect of prior probability / false positive fallacy)
parsed.explanation: The reasoning focuses entirely on the 95% sensitivity (true positive rate) while ignoring the base rate of the condition in the screened population. In any low-prevalence condition, even a 95% sensitivity combined with an unspecified specificity can produce a majority of false positives. For example, if prevalence is 1% and specificity is 95%, roughly half of all positive tests would be false positives. Without knowing the base rate and specificity, the 95% figure is insufficient to justify a blanket 'strong endorsement' — yet the reasoning treats it as decisive evidence of value.
